item.title: The Grand Canal, Venice, Looking South toward the Rialto Bridge
item.museum: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
item.lesson: Begin with a question that will occupy us for eleven mornings: can light on water be painted? Canaletto's answer was yes — with enough discipline. He built Venice the way a watchmaker builds a movement: every palace facade plumb, every gondola's shadow accounted for, the Grand Canal opening toward the Rialto Bridge in perspective so exact that scholars still argue over whether he used a camera obscura, a lens-and-mirror device that projects a scene onto paper for tracing. His clients were mostly English aristocrats finishing the Grand Tour, and what they bought was proof: Venice as it verifiably, measurably was. Look at the water. It is a floor — a green-glass pavement that politely holds the reflections it is issued and does not move. Light, in Canaletto, is inventory. Everything it touches is listed, priced, and delivered. It took the next century and a half to discover how much that certainty left out.
item.lookFor: Find the small figures leaning from balconies and gondolas — each one crisp, individual, and utterly still, as if the entire city were holding its breath for the camera.
